---
title: "Executive Summary"
---


::: {.callout-tip icon=false}
## The One-Paragraph Version

We built **Cedant**, a local-first cited-answer copilot for
reinsurance underwriting research. Given a question and an
indexed corpus of public regulatory and corporate documents (PRA,
EIOPA, Munich Re, Swiss Re), Cedant retrieves the relevant
document chunks using hybrid dense-and-sparse search, then asks a
local language model to write an answer in which every factual
claim is paired with a structural citation to a specific chunk.
Citations are validated by set-membership against the retrieved
context, hallucinated citations are partitioned out and surfaced
explicitly, and out-of-corpus questions trigger an exact-phrase
refusal rather than a fabricated answer. Cedant is a five-day
interview artefact, not a production system; everything runs on a
single laptop with no third-party API calls at any pipeline stage,
including the evaluation. The 70-question hand-crafted benchmark
is scored deterministically against gold chunk references —
structurally, not by an LLM judge. **The contribution is less a
product than a discipline: a small, locally reproducible RAG
system in which the citation contract and the refusal contract are
first-class, structurally enforced, and measured against
pre-stated falsification criteria.**
:::

---

## The Problem {#sec-exec-problem}

Reinsurance underwriting research is, in large part, document
work. An underwriter assessing a property catastrophe risk, a
casualty treaty, or an ESG exclusion will draw on regulatory
supervisory statements, internal underwriting manuals, peer
reinsurers' published sustainability reports, broker submissions,
and modelling-firm white papers. The synthesis is manual,
repetitive, and time-pressured.

The core difficulty is not the volume of documents but the
**citation discipline** the work demands. Underwriters cannot rely
on a free-form summary of what some document "probably says" —
they need to know exactly which paragraph of which supervisory
statement supports a position, because that paragraph may need to
be cited in an internal committee paper or a regulator
submission. A tool that synthesises plausibly but cites loosely
or hallucinates is worse than no tool: it shifts the verification
burden onto the user under time pressure and creates a
confident-sounding artefact that the user must now disprove.

Three related concerns shape the design of any practical tool in
this space:

- **Data sensitivity.** Underwriting documents include internal
  pricing rationale, broker submissions under non-disclosure, and
  competitive intelligence. A system that sends queries or
  retrieved chunks to a third-party API is, for many reinsurers,
  a non-starter.

- **Source verifiability.** A summary without a clearly-resolved
  citation to a specific paragraph is not actionable. The system
  must distinguish between *what the corpus says* and *what the
  model thinks the corpus says*, and must refuse rather than
  fabricate when the corpus does not contain the answer.

- **Evaluation honesty.** A useful evaluation must include
  out-of-corpus questions, must report hallucination counts
  rather than smoothing them, and must be reproducible by a
  reader who has not seen the eval pipeline.

These three concerns — local execution, structural citation
discipline, and an honest evaluation harness — are the design
constraints from which Cedant follows.

---

## What We Built {#sec-exec-what}

Cedant is a single-machine RAG pipeline whose architecture is
intentionally small.

```
User question
      │
      ▼
┌──────────────────────────┐
│  Hybrid Retrieval        │  BGE-M3 dense embeddings + BM25 sparse,
│  (Qdrant + custom BM25)  │  fused via Reciprocal Rank Fusion (k=60)
└────────────┬─────────────┘
             │ top-k chunks (default k=5)
             ▼
┌──────────────────────────┐
│  Answer Generation       │  Local oMLX, OpenAI-compatible chat
│  (Gemma 4 31B IT default)│  completions, temperature 0
└────────────┬─────────────┘
             │ answer text with [chunk_id] citations
             ▼
┌──────────────────────────┐
│  Citation Validation     │  Regex extracts every [chunk_id];
│  + Refusal Detector      │  set-membership check against retrieved
│                          │  set; hallucinated citations partitioned
│                          │  out; exact-phrase refusal detected
└────────────┬─────────────┘
             ▼
   AnswerResult: cited answer  OR  refusal  +  source cards
```

**Retrieval.** The 461-chunk corpus is indexed twice: a dense
BGE-M3 embedding index (1024-dim, CLS-pooled and L2-normalised
per D010) in a local Qdrant store, and a sparse BM25 index over
a Porter-stemmed corpus vocabulary (~4,810 terms per D011). At
query time both retrievers run, and their results are fused via
Reciprocal Rank Fusion (k=60) using Qdrant's native hybrid query
API. Hybrid retrieval was chosen over pure dense or pure sparse
because regulatory and corporate documents contain both rare
named entities (specific paragraph numbers, regulation
identifiers) where BM25 dominates, and free-form descriptive
prose where dense embeddings dominate.

**Answer generation.** The retrieved chunks are passed to a local
language model served via oMLX on `http://127.0.0.1:8000/v1`.
The production default is **Gemma 4 31B IT 6-bit MLX**, selected
under D015 on the basis of the N=70 evaluation (hallucination
floor at zero, citation recall identical under both prompt
versions, small but consistent within-document advantage).
Qwen3.6 35B-A3B 4-bit remains available as a latency-budget
alternative (approximately 6.7× faster) via the
`UNDERWRITING_COPILOT_MODEL` environment variable. Temperature is
hard-pinned to 0.0; same query plus same corpus plus same model
plus same prompt produces the same answer.

**Citation validation.** A regular expression
(`\[([A-Za-z0-9_\-]+)\]`) matches every `[chunk_id]` token in
the model's output and checks each against the set of retrieved
chunk identifiers. Citations that point at chunks not in the
retrieved set are classified as **hallucinated citations** and
surfaced explicitly in the user interface as red `[?]` badges,
rather than silently allowed to mislead the reader. The
partition into valid and hallucinated is structural; the
detector cannot be tricked by reformatting.

**Refusal contract.** When retrieval surfaces no relevant chunks,
or when the model determines the retrieved chunks do not contain
the answer, the system commits to emitting the exact phrase
*"I cannot answer this from the provided sources."* The phrase
is detected by strict string match (after stripping trailing
whitespace and end-of-sentence punctuation). Partial refusals
are rejected; the detector cannot be subverted by wrapping the
refusal phrase around an unauthorised answer. When triggered,
the answer card is replaced by a dedicated refusal card and the
retrieved chunks (if any) are still shown so the user can verify
the refusal was sound.

**Analyst surface.** A local Streamlit application renders cited
answers inline, with citations as green badges that link to
source cards below. Hallucinated citations render in red.
Sample-query exemplars demonstrate the canonical query shapes
(single-document regulatory, single-document corporate,
cross-document synthesis, out-of-corpus refusal). The same
`Retriever` and `AnswerGenerator` objects that the Streamlit UI
constructs are the ones the evaluation harness uses; behaviour
observed in the UI is the behaviour the eval measures.

---

## Key Results {#sec-exec-results}

::: {.callout-note}
## Evaluation methodology

Numbers below come from the canonical N=70 evaluation run at
`eval/results/2026-06-18T15-32-07Z/` — 44 answerable + 26 refusal
questions, scored across a 2×2 sweep of two models × two prompt
versions (280 cells total). Scoring is **structural**: citation
recall is the fraction of gold chunks the model cited; refusal
correctness is exact-phrase match. There is no LLM-as-judge layer
in the v1 eval (Q10.3, deferred to v2). The full sweep ran in
45.7 minutes wall-clock with zero errored cells.
:::

### Production-default cell (Gemma 4 31B IT × prompt v2)

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Questions evaluated | 70 (44 answerable + 26 refusal) |
| Mean citation recall, all answerable | **0.598** |
| Mean citation recall, excluding retrieval misses | **0.798** |
| Mean citation recall, single-chunk retrievable (n=17) | **1.000** |
| Mean citation recall, cross-document (n=10) | 0.233 |
| Refusal correctness | **26/26 (100%)** across all 3 categories |
| Hallucinated citations across full answerable sweep | **0** |
| Mean wall-clock, answerable | 22.9 s |
| Mean wall-clock, refusal | 8.6 s |

: Production-default cell headline numbers, N=70.
{#tbl-exec-leaderboard}

### Five qualitative findings the evaluation supports

1. **The citation contract holds for the production-default
   model.** Gemma × v2 produced zero hallucinated citations across
   the 44-question answerable sweep. Every emitted `[chunk_id]`
   resolves to a chunk that was in the retrieved context.
   Qwen × v2 produced 7 hallucinations across the same sweep
   (down from 23 under v1, a 3.3× reduction under the better
   prompt). The structural validator partitions both fairly; the
   choice between models is therefore explicit on the
   hallucination axis.

2. **The refusal contract holds for both models.** Every cell of
   the sweep refuses every question in the 26-question refusal
   benchmark — 104 of 104 refusal-cell outcomes correct, across
   three adversarial categories (out-of-corpus, adjacent-but-
   unanswered, false-premise). No partial refusals occurred. The
   strict exact-phrase detector did not need to fall back on
   tolerance.

3. **The system's weakness is upstream of the language model.**
   25% of answerable questions (11 of 44) fail at the retrieval
   stage across all four cells — the gold chunks were never in
   the top-5, regardless of model or prompt. Q12 traced the root
   cause to query/chunk language asymmetry on single-vector dense
   embeddings; remediation paths (HyDE, cross-encoder reranker,
   BGE-M3 multi-vector) are deferred to v2 as Q13. The 0.798
   `excluding_retrieval_misses` number is the answer-stage
   ceiling; the 0.598 headline is what retrieval drags it down to.

4. **Cross-document synthesis is the system's clearest weakness.**
   The cross_document subset (n=10) scores below 0.25 mean
   citation_recall in every cell, including the production
   default at 0.233. `docs/governance.md` commits to the system
   not being relied on for cross-document synthesis as a primary
   use case. The N=10 cross-document subset is what underwrites
   that commitment.

5. **Two claims have been retracted on the record.** A Day-2
   family-axis citation-discipline claim retracted after the N=40
   D014 sweep showed Qwen v2 closed the gap to Gemma to within
   3.2 percentage points (against a pre-stated 10pp threshold).
   A Day-4 within-document parity claim weakened (not retracted)
   when the N=70 extension showed a small but consistent ~5pp
   Gemma advantage that the N=40 partition had not surfaced.
   Both retractions are recorded in `docs/journal.md` by
   timestamp and commit hash, append-only. The discipline of
   pre-stating the criterion and retracting when the evidence
   demanded it is part of the artefact, not a footnote to it.

---

## What This Is Not {#sec-exec-not}

Cedant is a **five-day interview artefact**, not a production
system. The following limitations are constitutive, not bugs.

- **The corpus is small and curated.** Six PDFs from four issuers
  (PRA × 3 supervisory statements, EIOPA × 1, Munich Re × 1,
  Swiss Re × 1), 461 chunks after preprocessing. Three synthetic
  Sycamore Reinsurance documents are drafted to demonstrate the
  pipeline structurally extends to internal-category content, but
  are **not indexed in v1**; the eval numbers are reported
  against the 4-issuer real corpus only. Performance on this
  corpus does not predict performance on a production-scale
  underwriting document store.

- **Evaluation is structural, not semantic.** The eval measures
  whether the model cited the chunks the benchmark author
  designated as gold. It does **not** measure whether the
  model's prose accurately reflects what those chunks say. An
  LLM-as-judge semantic-correctness layer is recorded as Q10.3
  and deferred to v2. Until that lands, headline citation recall
  should be read as a structural proxy for semantic correctness,
  not as semantic correctness itself.

- **Single-seed runs; no variance estimates.** Each cell of the
  sweep is one run. The qualitative findings are robust to any
  plausible single-seed variance, but precise digits past the
  first decimal should be read with that caveat. Multi-seed
  variance is recorded as v2 Priority 3.

- **No adversarial corpus or red-team eval.** The 26-question
  refusal benchmark covers out-of-corpus, adjacent-but-
  unanswered, and false-premise questions, but does not include
  prompt-injection attacks, jailbreaks, or adversarially-
  rephrased questions designed to defeat the citation contract.

- **No real underwriter has used it.** The Streamlit interface
  has not been tested in a real underwriting workflow. The
  sample-query exemplars exist precisely because the interface
  has not yet been exercised against the variability of real
  analyst queries.

- **Not validated against legal or compliance requirements.**
  Cedant retrieves and cites public documents; it does not
  constitute legal, regulatory, or underwriting advice and has
  not been reviewed by compliance.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Production Readiness
Cedant is a research artefact built in five days. It should not
be used for live underwriting decisions, regulatory submissions,
or any work product where citation correctness has external
consequences.
:::

---

## Project at a Glance {#sec-exec-glance}

| | |
|---|---|
| **Domain** | Reinsurance underwriting research |
| **Indexed corpus** | 6 PDFs from 4 issuers (PRA × 3, EIOPA × 1, Munich Re × 1, Swiss Re × 1); 15.3 MB; 461 chunks |
| **Synthetic carve-out** | 3 Sycamore Reinsurance documents drafted, not indexed in v1 |
| **Retrieval** | Hybrid BGE-M3 dense (CLS+L2-normalised) + BM25 sparse (Porter-stemmed, ~4,810 terms), fused via RRF k=60; top_k=5 |
| **Vector store** | Local Qdrant (filesystem-backed) |
| **Generation** | Local oMLX, OpenAI-compatible chat completions; temperature 0.0; production default Gemma 4 31B IT 6-bit MLX (D015) |
| **Alternate model** | Qwen3.6 35B-A3B 4-bit, overridable via `UNDERWRITING_COPILOT_MODEL` env var |
| **Citation contract** | `[chunk_id]` regex, set-membership against retrieved set; hallucinated citations partitioned out and surfaced |
| **Refusal contract** | Exact-phrase match: *"I cannot answer this from the provided sources."* Partial refusals rejected |
| **Analyst surface** | Local Streamlit application; green citation badges; red `[?]` for hallucinated citations; source cards; dedicated refusal card |
| **Evaluation** | N=70 hand-crafted benchmark (44 answerable + 26 refusal); 2×2 sweep (models × prompts); structural scoring; deterministic report regeneration |
| **Production-default headline** | 0.598 citation_recall all-answerable; 0.798 excluding retrieval misses; 1.000 on single-chunk-retrievable; **26/26** refusal; **0** hallucinations |
| **Headline weakness** | Cross-document subset: 0.233 on production default; retrieval miss rate 25% (Q13 v2 work-stream) |
| **Latency** | Gemma 22.9 s answerable / 8.6 s refusal · Qwen 3.4 s / 0.9 s |
| **Determinism** | Same software state → same `raw.jsonl` and `report.md` to the bit |
| **Reproducibility** | Two canonical eval runs committed (`eval/results/2026-06-18T*Z/`); regenerable via `uv run python -m eval.runner` and `uv run python -m eval.report` |
| **Hardware** | MacBook Pro M5 Max, 128 GB unified memory |
| **Codebase** | Python 3.14, Streamlit, Qdrant, oMLX, `uv` for packaging |
| **Tests** | 177+ unit and integration tests (158+ pipeline, 19 Streamlit AppTest) |
| **Build window** | Five days, end-to-end |
| **Retractions on record** | 2 — family-axis citation discipline (N=40); within-document parity (N=70 weakening) |
| **Scope** | Interview artefact / proof of concept · not a production system |
